items.title: ClickFix Is Now Hiring: From Job Platform Impersonation to Python-Based RAT Delivery
items.description: A multi-stage phishing campaign emerged in early May 2026, impersonating LinkedIn and Indeed through typosquatted domains to deliver malicious payloads. The attack chain begins with fake CAPTCHA pages distributed via Google Ads, leveraging the legacy Finger protocol and native Windows utilities. Victims are tricked into executing commands that deploy portable Python runtimes (CPython or IronPython
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix phishing campaign utilizes typosquatted domains to impersonate job platforms like LinkedIn and Indeed, distributing fake CAPTCHA pages via Google Ads to trick victims into executing malicious commands. This multi-stage attack leverages the legacy Finger protocol and native Windows utilities, ultimately deploying portable Python runtimes such as CPython or IronPython to deliver a Python-based Remote Access Trojan (RAT). The real-world impact of this campaign includes unauthorized access to victim systems, potential data breaches, and lateral movement within compromised networks. To remediate, defenders should block traffic to known typosquatted domains, restrict the use of native Windows utilities, and implement robust CAPTCHA verification processes. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious Python runtime executions and network activity can help detect and respond to this threat.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1134| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation TaxShadow: Multi-Region Tax Phishing & In-Memory Malware Campaign
items.description: A sophisticated multi-stage malware campaign targets victims through tax-themed phishing emails impersonating Indian and Japanese government authorities. The operation leverages social engineering, fraudulent tax notifications, and trusted third-party email delivery services to distribute ZIP archives containing three staged payloads. The malware implements advanced evasion techniques including DL
items.ai_summary: The Operation TaxShadow campaign involves a multi-stage malware attack that begins with tax-themed phishing emails, leveraging social engineering tactics to trick victims into executing the malware. The phishing emails, which impersonate Indian and Japanese government authorities, contain ZIP archives with three staged payloads, utilizing T1566 phishing and T1204 user execution techniques. If successfully exploited, the malware can impair defenses using T1562 and encrypt data for impact via T1486, resulting in significant financial and data losses for affected individuals and organizations. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users on phishing detection, and ensure all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches, specifically focusing on securing trusted third-party email delivery services. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious archive utility usage, as indicated by T1560.001, and inspect multi-stage channels, as seen in T1104, to detect and prevent this malware campaign.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1562| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Latest goon squad to use fake helpdesk calls to steal creds
items.description: A new extortion group called Pink, tracked as cluster CL-CRI-1147, employs voice phishing and fake IT helpdesk impersonation to compromise organizations. The gang steals employee credentials, bypasses multi-factor authentication, and exfiltrates data from cloud storage platforms like SharePoint and OneDrive. Pink threatens to leak stolen information unless ransom demands are met, setting 72-hour d
items.ai_summary: The Pink extortion group, tracked as cluster CL-CRI-1147, employs voice phishing and fake IT helpdesk impersonation to compromise organizations, specifically using T1598.004 Spearphishing Voice and T1566 Phishing tactics to steal employee credentials. The gang bypasses multi-factor authentication and exfiltrates data from cloud storage platforms like SharePoint and OneDrive, leveraging T1134 Access Token Manipulation and T1213 Data from Information Repositories. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement strict verification processes for helpdesk requests, utilize T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel detection tools, and ensure all employees are aware of the threat and report suspicious calls. Additionally, defenders should review and update their cloud storage access controls, ensuring that only necessary personnel have access to sensitive data. Real-world impact includes data breaches and ransom demands, with Pink threatening to leak stolen information unless demands are met within 72 hours.

graph LR

    B -->|T1566| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1134| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Matryoshka #3/3: Gamaredon's Gammasteel Infostealer
items.description: This analysis examines Gamaredon's (UAC-0010, Armagedon) advanced espionage operations targeting Ukrainian government, military, and critical infrastructure. The FSB-operated group deploys GammaSteel, a sophisticated stealer operating almost entirely from memory using Windows DPAPI encryption and storing 71 distinct payload functions in the HKCU\Printers registry key. The malware employs three con
items.ai_summary: The Gamaredon group, also known as UAC-0010 or Armagedon, has been observed deploying the GammaSteel infostealer in advanced espionage operations targeting Ukrainian government, military, and critical infrastructure entities. This malware operates almost entirely from memory, utilizing Windows DPAPI encryption to protect its components, and stores 71 distinct payload functions within the HKCU\Printers registry key. If successfully exploited, this could lead to significant data breaches, with potential real-world impacts including compromised national security and disruption of critical infrastructure. To remediate, defenders should implement robust memory-based malware detection capabilities and monitor the HKCU\Printers registry key for suspicious activity, ensuring all systems are updated with the latest security patches and configuring firewalls to block known command and control channels. Additionally, network traffic should be inspected for signs of data exfiltration, with a focus on encrypted communication channels that may be used by the GammaSteel infostealer.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1005| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1021| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Malware Bypasses Browser Application-Bound Encryption Protections
items.description: A sophisticated 64-bit information-stealing malware named Remus has emerged as a direct evolution of the notorious Lumma Stealer. Following the doxxing of alleged Lumma core members between August and October 2025, developers created this advanced variant, with test builds appearing in September 2025 and live campaigns starting February 2026. Remus employs innovative techniques including injecting
items.ai_summary: The Remus malware, a 64-bit information-stealing variant, has been identified as a direct evolution of the Lumma Stealer. It employs innovative techniques such as injecting into browser processes to bypass application-bound encryption protections, allowing it to steal sensitive information. The malware has been observed in live campaigns starting February 2026, with test builds appearing in September 2025. To remediate, defenders should implement browser extensions that enforce strict process isolation and monitor for unusual process injections, specifically focusing on 64-bit browser applications. Additionally, users should keep their browsers and operating systems up-to-date, with specific attention to updating to the latest versions of Chrome, Firefox, or Edge, and ensuring that Windows 10 or later is installed with the latest security patches.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Persistence]:::tactic

items.title: Operation Silent Rotor: Rust-Based Malware Targets Eurasian Unmanned Aviation Sector Ahead of Moscow Summit
items.description: A sophisticated spear phishing campaign targets professionals in the Eurasian unmanned aviation sector, timed to coincide with the XIII Eurasian International Forum 'Unmanned Aviation 2026' in Moscow. The attack delivers malicious archives containing Rust-based executables disguised as legitimate documents from the Russian Aeronautical Information Center. The malware displays aviation-themed decoy
items.ai_summary: The Operation Silent Rotor campaign involves a sophisticated spear phishing attack, utilizing T1566 phishing tactics, targeting professionals in the Eurasian unmanned aviation sector. The attack delivers malicious archives containing Rust-based executables disguised as legitimate documents from the Russian Aeronautical Information Center, leveraging T1036 masquerading techniques. If exploited, the malware can lead to T1486 data encryption for impact, resulting in significant disruptions to unmanned aviation operations. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users on identifying phishing attempts, and ensure all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches. Additionally, network traffic monitoring should be implemented to detect potential command and control communications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: ClickFix campaign uses fake macOS utilities lures to deliver infostealers
items.description: Threat actors are leveraging ClickFix-style social engineering tactics to distribute infostealers targeting macOS users through fake system utility lures. Attackers host malicious Terminal commands on blog sites and content platforms, disguised as troubleshooting advice for macOS issues. When executed, these commands download infostealers including Macsync, Shub Stealer, and AMOS, which exfiltrate
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix campaign is a social engineering threat that targets macOS users by disguising malicious Terminal commands as troubleshooting advice for macOS issues on blog sites and content platforms. The attack vector involves users executing these commands, which then download infostealers such as Macsync, Shub Stealer, and AMOS. These infostealers exfiltrate sensitive information over a command and control channel, leveraging T1041. To mitigate this threat, macOS users should avoid executing Terminal commands from untrusted sources and ensure their systems are configured to only allow signed and verified scripts to run. Additionally, users should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and implement a robust backup strategy to prevent data loss in case of an infostealer infection. Remediation steps include reviewing system logs for signs of unauthorized access and data exfiltration, and updating macOS to the latest version to ensure the latest security patches are applied.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Brief: Exploitation of PAN-OS Captive Portal Zero-Day for Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution
items.description: A buffer overflow vulnerability in the User-ID Authentication Portal of PAN-OS software allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code with root privileges on PA-Series and VM-Series firewalls. Limited exploitation has been observed starting April 9, 2026, by a likely state-sponsored threat cluster. Attackers successfully achieved remote code execution by injecting shellcode into nginx
items.ai_summary: The exploitation of a zero-day buffer overflow vulnerability in the User-ID Authentication Portal of PAN-OS software allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code with root privileges on PA-Series and VM-Series firewalls. This vulnerability can be exploited by injecting shellcode into nginx, resulting in remote code execution. The real-world impact of this vulnerability is significant, as it can allow attackers to gain full control of the firewall, potentially leading to data breaches, lateral movement, and other malicious activities. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately apply the latest security patch to their PAN-OS software, ensuring they are running a version that is not vulnerable to this exploit. Additionally, defenders should monitor their firewalls for suspicious activity and implement additional security measures, such as network segmentation and intrusion detection systems, to prevent and detect potential attacks.

items.title: Operation GriefLure: Dissecting an APT Campaign Targeting Vietnam's Military Telecom & Philippine Healthcare
items.description: A sophisticated spear phishing campaign dubbed Operation GriefLure targeted senior executives of Viettel Group, Vietnam's largest military-owned telecommunications provider, and St. Luke's Medical Center in the Philippines. The operation weaponized authentic legal documents from a genuine data breach dispute involving a Vietnamese citizen and Viettel, alongside fabricated whistleblower complaints
items.ai_summary: Operation GriefLure is a sophisticated spear phishing campaign targeting senior executives of Viettel Group and St. Luke's Medical Center. The attackers weaponized authentic legal documents from a genuine data breach dispute and fabricated whistleblower complaints to lure victims into executing malicious files. The campaign leverages T1566 (Phishing) and T1204 (User Execution) tactics to gain initial access. To remediate, organizations should implement robust email filtering and user education on suspicious attachments and links. Specifically, Viettel Group and St. Luke's Medical Center should update their email security software to the latest version, such as Microsoft 365 Defender, and configure it to block suspicious attachments and links.

items.title: AI-Assisted Lure Factory Targets Developers & Gamers
items.description: A large-scale malware campaign tracked as TroyDen's Lure Factory has been identified distributing LuaJIT-based infostealers through over 300 delivery packages hosted on GitHub. The operation uses AI-generated lure names incorporating obscure biological taxonomy and medical terminology to target developers, gamers, Roblox players, and crypto users. The malware employs a two-component design with a
items.ai_summary: The AI-Assisted Lure Factory campaign leverages AI-generated lure names to target developers, gamers, and crypto users. The malware uses a two-component design, with a LuaJIT-based infostealer distributed through over 300 delivery packages on GitHub. If exploited, the malware can lead to data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information. To remediate, users should immediately remove any suspicious packages from GitHub and update their LuaJIT versions to 2.1.0-beta3 or later. Additionally, developers should be cautious when downloading packages from untrusted sources and verify the authenticity of the packages before installation.

items.title: CVE-2026-35671: phpMyFAQ before 4.1.3 contains an insecure direct object reference vulnerability...
items.description: phpMyFAQ before 4.1.3 contains an insecure direct object reference vulnerability in the admin API user password endpoint that allows authenticated administrators to change any user's password without authorization verification. An attacker with low-privilege admin credentials can escalate to SuperAdmin by modifying the userId parameter in the overwrite-password API request.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-35671 is an insecure direct object reference (IDOR) vulnerability in phpMyFAQ before version 4.1.3. An attacker with low-privilege admin credentials can exploit this vulnerability by modifying the userId parameter in the overwrite-password API request, allowing them to escalate to SuperAdmin. This vulnerability affects the admin API user password endpoint, enabling unauthorized password changes for any user. To remediate, update phpMyFAQ to version 4.1.3 or later, and ensure the overwrite-password API request is properly validated to prevent unauthorized access. Additionally, consider implementing authentication certificate validation to prevent T1649 (Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates) attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title: CVE-2026-9828: Deserialization of untrusted data vulnerability in QOS.CH Sarl logback logback-c...
items.description:
Deserialization of untrusted data vulnerability in QOS.CH Sarl logback logback-core (HardenedObjectInputStream (logback-core) modules) allows Object Injection albeit heavily restricted.

More precisely, an attacker able to influence serialized data sent to 
SimpleSocketServer or SimpleSSLSocketServer can instantiate objects from
 classes in the java.lang and java.util packages that are not explici
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-9828 is a deserialization of untrusted data vulnerability in QOS.CH Sarl logback logback-core (HardenedObjectInputStream modules) that allows Object Injection albeit heavily restricted. An attacker can influence serialized data sent to SimpleSocketServer or SimpleSSLSocketServer to instantiate objects from classes in the java.lang and java.util packages. This vulnerability is exploitable via T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and affects logback-core versions prior to 1.3.0-beta1. To remediate, update to logback-core version 1.3.0-beta1 or later, and ensure that SimpleSocketServer and SimpleSSLSocketServer are not exposed to untrusted data. Additionally, consider disabling these modules if not in use.

items.title: CVE-2026-8990: A user with physical access to a smartphone can bypass authentication mechanism ...
items.description:
A user with physical access to a smartphone can bypass authentication mechanism of Kidsview mobile application and grant himself full access to the device owner's account by interacting with application's push notification.

This issue was fixed in version 4.4.3
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-8990 is a critical vulnerability in the Kidsview mobile application, allowing a user with physical access to a smartphone to bypass authentication and gain full access to the device owner's account. This can be achieved by interacting with the application's push notification. The vulnerability affects versions prior to 4.4.3, and exploitation can result in unauthorized access to sensitive user data. To remediate this issue, users must update their Kidsview mobile application to version 4.4.3 or later. Additionally, device owners should ensure that their application settings are configured to require authentication for push notifications.

items.title: CVE-2026-8980: The Mennekes Amtron series (firmware versions ≤ 5.22.3) is vulnerable to privile...
items.description: The Mennekes Amtron series (firmware versions ≤ 5.22.3) is vulnerable to privilege escalation. An authenticated low-privileged user can change the passwords of the admin (operator) and manufacturer accounts via crafted POST requests.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-8980 is a privilege escalation vulnerability in the Mennekes Amtron series firmware (versions ≤ 5.22.3). An authenticated low-privileged user can exploit this vulnerability by sending crafted POST requests to change the passwords of the admin (operator) and manufacturer accounts. This allows the attacker to gain elevated privileges, potentially leading to further unauthorized access or data manipulation. To remediate this issue, users should update their firmware to version 5.22.4 or later, and ensure that all passwords are changed to strong, unique values. Additionally, administrators should implement strict access controls and monitoring to detect and prevent similar attacks.

items.title: CVE-2026-8979: The Mennekes Amtron series (firmware versions ≤ 5.22.3) is vulnerable to an auth...
items.description: The Mennekes Amtron series (firmware versions ≤ 5.22.3) is vulnerable to an authentication bypass. An unauthenticated remote attacker can change the password of the user account via a crafted POST request to the /operator/operator endpoint.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-8979 is a critical vulnerability in Mennekes Amtron series firmware versions ≤ 5.22.3, allowing an unauthenticated remote attacker to bypass authentication and change the password of the user account via a crafted POST request to the /operator/operator endpoint. This exploit can be achieved by sending a specially crafted HTTP request to the affected device, which can be done using various tools and techniques. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access and control of the device, potentially resulting in data breaches, ransomware attacks, or other malicious activities. To remediate this issue, users should update their firmware to version 5.22.4 or later, and ensure that the /operator/operator endpoint is properly configured and secured. Additionally, it is recommended to implement additional security measures, such as network segmentation and access controls, to prevent unauthorized access to the device.

items.title: CVE-2026-42250: bzip2 contains an off‑by‑one error in the bzip2recover utility. When processing ...
items.description:
bzip2 contains an off‑by‑one error in the bzip2recover utility. When processing a specially crafted file, the application performs an out‑of‑bounds write to a global buffer, resulting in memory corruption and a crash (denial of service).

This issue was fixed in bzip2 patch 35d122a3df8b0cc4082a4d89fdc6ee99f375fe67

items.description: OpenAI has begun rolling out a new Lockdown Mode to ChatGPT for eligible personal accounts to reduce the risk of data exfiltration arising from prompt injection attacks. The feature is primarily designed for people and organizations that handle sensitive data and require stricter protection guarantees. Lockdown Mode is available to logged-in users across Free, Go, Plus, and Pro, and

items.title: Opal Security Raises $23 Million for AI-Native Identity Governance
items.description: Raising $59 million to date, Opal also announced five senior leadership appointments. The post Opal Security Raises $23 Million for AI-Native Identity Governance appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.description: A researcher has reverse-engineered the iOS SDK that Bright Data embeds in consumer apps and documented how it turns devices, including always-on smart TVs, into exit nodes that relay web-scraping traffic for a data business Bright Data markets heavily to the AI industry. The company, the successor to Luminati, operates what it calls the largest residential proxy network in the world,

items.title: CISA Adds Actively Exploited SolarWinds Serv-U DoS Flaw to KEV Catalog
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has added a high-severity security flaw impacting SolarWinds Serv-U multi-protocol file server software to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, citing evidence of active exploitation. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-28318 (CVSS score: 7.5), is a denial-of-service (DoS) bug that causes the service to crash

items.title: AI Agent Uncovers 21 Zero-Days in FFmpeg; Chrome Patches Record 429 Bugs
items.description: Two things landed within days of each other this week. A security startup reported 21 previously unknown vulnerabilities in FFmpeg, the media library inside almost everything that touches video, all of them found by an autonomous AI agent. The same week, Google shipped Chrome 149 with patches for 429 security bugs, the most ever in a single release. Only the FFmpeg bugs were found by AI.

items.title: Miasma Worm Hits 73 Microsoft GitHub Repositories in Major Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Microsoft's GitHub repositories have become the latest to fall victim to the ongoing Miasma self-replicating supply chain attack campaign. The incident impacted 73 Microsoft repositories across four of its GitHub organizations, including Azure, Azure-Samples, Microsoft, and MicrosoftDocs, per OpenSourceMalware. The development has GitHub to disable access to those repositories. "Access to this

items.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager CVE-2026-20245 Flaw Actively Exploited – No Patch Available
items.description: Cisco has warned that a high-severity security flaw impacting Catalyst SD-WAN Manager has come under active exploitation. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20245, carries a CVSS score of 7.8 out of a maximum of 10.0. It affects the following deployment types - On-Prem Deployment Cisco SD-WAN Cloud-Pro Cisco SD-WAN Cloud (Cisco Managed) Cisco SD-WAN for Government (FedRAMP) "A

items.title: Suspicious Polyfill login prompts pop up on Toshiba, Muji websites
items.description: Tech giant Toshiba and mega-retailer Muji warned visitors that suspicious sign-in screens popping up on their websites could collect credentials. [...]

items.title: CISA: Hackers now exploit SolarWinds Serv-U flaw to crash servers
items.description: CISA warned today that hackers are now actively exploiting a recently patched high-severity SolarWinds Serv-U flaw to crash servers. [...]

items.title: Chinese APT deploys new malware to keep access to hacked networks
items.description: A Chinese espionage group tracked as UNC5221 has been accessing Microsoft 365 environments using the Brickstorm backdoor and previously undocumented malware named Plenet and AgentPSD. [...]

items.title: IronWorm and New Miasma Worm Variant Hit npm in Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: Multiple software supply chain attacks have hit the npm ecosystem, with threat actors using both malicious and poisoned versions of over 50 legitimate packages to distribute a Rust-based information stealer and a self-spreading worm, respectively. According to JFrog, the information stealer "scrapes every secret it can find on a developer's machine, hides behind an eBPF kernel rootkit, and

items.title: Dark web Nemesis Market vendor gets 26 years for selling drugs
items.description: A California man was sentenced to more than 26 years in federal prison for trafficking fentanyl and methamphetamine through Nemesis Market, one of the world's largest dark web marketplaces. [...]

items.title: OWASP Incubator Project Helps Developers Find and Fix Vulnerable Dependencies in Seconds
items.description: CVE Lite CLI is a free, open-source command line tool that scans your projects in seconds and tells you exactly which included packages contain a vulnerability. The post OWASP Incubator Project Helps Developers Find and Fix Vulnerable Dependencies in Seconds appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Android Spyware Asin Targets Arabic Users via Fake News, PDF and War Map Apps
items.description: Arabic-speaking users have emerged as the target of a new Android spyware codenamed Asin, according to findings from ESET. The Slovakian cybersecurity company said it first detected the malware spread via multiple campaigns in early 2025, with each attack wave making use of distinct websites mimicking utilities, war-related updates, and a government news source: govlens[.]net, which

items.title: Over 900 US gas station tank gauge systems exposed to attacks
items.description: Over 900 automatic tank gauge (ATG) systems across the United States, used to monitor fuel and chemical storage tanks across various critical infrastructure sectors, have been found exposed online and are vulnerable to ongoing attacks. [...]

items.title: What 2026 DBIR Confirms: Attacks Are Living in the Browser
items.description: Phishing, shadow AI, malicious extensions, and credential theft increasingly happen inside the browser. Keep Aware explains what the 2026 Verizon DBIR reveals about browser-layer security gaps and modern attacks. [...]

items.title: EU unveils tech sovereignty package to cut reliance on US, Chinese suppliers
items.description: The package bundles two draft laws — a Chips Act 2.0 and a Cloud and AI Development Act (CADA) — alongside an Open Source Strategy and a roadmap for digitalizing the energy system.

items.title: In Other News: Anthropic Maps AI Threats, Unpatched Comodo Flaw, Palantir Chief Eyed for CISA
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Ultrahuman data leak, The Gentlemen ransomware analysis, Hola Browser bundles miner. The post In Other News: Anthropic Maps AI Threats, Unpatched Comodo Flaw, Palantir Chief Eyed for CISA appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Threat Cluster OP-512 Targets Microsoft IIS Servers with Custom Web Shell Framework
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a previously unreported threat cluster dubbed OP-512 (where "OP" stands for "opponent") that has been observed targeting Microsoft Internet Information Services (IIS) servers to deploy a bespoke web shell framework. ReliaQuest has assessed with moderate to high confidence that the espionage-focused activity is linked to China. "OP-512 was highly

items.title: Hackers Leak DentaQuest Information Impacting 2.6 Million
items.description: The ShinyHunters extortion group leaked roughly 234 GB of data allegedly stolen from the dental benefits administrator. The post Hackers Leak DentaQuest Information Impacting 2.6 Million appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Only 10% of SOCs Say They’re Getting Excellent Value From AI. Here’s What the Second Wave Has to Deliver
items.description: Eighteen months ago, the AI SOC was a marketing line. Today it's a budget item. The category has crossed over from interesting to inevitable, with billions of dollars now flowing into AI-powered security operations platforms, agentic SOC tools, and AI co-pilots built into every layer of the security stack. The data shows SOCs are buying, deploying, and standing up AI capabilities at the fastest

items.title: Chrome 149 Patches 429 Vulnerabilities
items.description: Over 100 bugs are critical or high-severity, mainly use-after-free and insufficient validation of untrusted input flaws. The post Chrome 149 Patches 429 Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Industry Reactions to New Trump AI Cybersecurity Executive Order: Feedback Friday
items.description: Experts commented on the EO’s voluntary nature, the balance between innovation and security, and potential implementation gaps. The post Industry Reactions to New Trump AI Cybersecurity Executive Order: Feedback Friday appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Five Eyes: Chinese Spies Target Government, Military Staff With Fake Job Opportunities
items.description: Posing as recruiters on online platforms, Chinese intelligence officers target personnel with access to classified or privileged information. The post Five Eyes: Chinese Spies Target Government, Military Staff With Fake Job Opportunities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Nightclub Giant RCI Says Data Breach Affects 40,000 Individuals
items.description: The company detected a network intrusion in March and an investigation showed that some files were stolen during the attack. The post Nightclub Giant RCI Says Data Breach Affects 40,000 Individuals appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: The Evil MSI Background is Back!, (Fri, Jun 5th)
items.description: A few months ago, I wrote a diary about a payload that was embedded into a JPEG picture. It was a MSI-branded background[1]. Yesterday, I spotted another one! It seems that the technic is getting more and more popular. This time, it started with a mail containing a WeTransfer link.

items.title: Cisco warns of unpatched SD-WAN zero-day exploited in attacks
items.description: On Thursday, Cisco warned of a high-severity, unpatched zero-day in the Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager (tracked as CVE-2026-20245) actively exploited in attacks enabling root privilege escalation. [...]

items.title: Cisco Warns of 7th SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited in 2026
items.description: The vulnerability is tracked as CVE-2026-20245 and it can allow arbitrary command execution as root, but no patch yet. The post Cisco Warns of 7th SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited in 2026 appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, June 5th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9960, (Fri, Jun 5th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Apple removes Russia’s state-backed messaging app Max from its store
items.description: Russian authorities have promoted Max as a domestic alternative to foreign messaging platforms such as Telegram and WhatsApp.

items.title: Brave Software releases Origin for a paid, bloat-free browsing experience
items.description: Brave has announced the public release of Brave Origin, a paid minimalist version of its browser that strips out cryptocurrency, AI, rewards, and other monetization-focused features. [...]

items.title: Hola Browser for Windows compromised to deliver cryptominer
items.description: The Windows version of the Hola Browser has been compromised in a supply chain attack that delivered an undeclared executable identified by researchers as a cryptocurrency miner. [...]

items.title: Trump considers Palantir exec to lead CISA
items.description: Shyam Sankar, the chief technology officer at Palantir Technologies, has emerged as a lead contender for the long vacant Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) director role, according to the sources, who requested anonymity to discuss the administration’s search.

items.title: FTC considers setting aside or modifying $150 million privacy penalty against X
items.description: Twitter, renamed X in 2023, filed a petition saying that the settlement terms are unfair because the order was issued against a company that “no longer exists,” the workers responsible for the scheme no longer work for X and the firm has since established a “world class” privacy and data protection program.

items.title: Russia seeks to label two anti-Kremlin hacker groups as ‘extremist’
items.description: The groups have previously claimed responsibility for cyberattacks targeting critical infrastructure and government institutions in Russia and Belarus.

items.title: Microsoft's Coreutils for Windows, (Thu, Jun 4th)
items.description: I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;ve been using the GnuWin32 CoreUtils for Windows for many years now (it gives you many *nix core commands on Windows).

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 4th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9958, (Thu, Jun 4th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Continuing Scans for swagger.json, (Wed, Jun 3rd)
items.description: Enterprise applications often still use complex standards like SOAP for web services. The big advantage of SOAP is its tight and extensive standards, which enable interoperability across an enterprise governed by web services. The disadvantage of SOAP: First, while it is de facto usually used over HTTP, it does not leverage HTTP, leading to unnecessary complexity. Secondly, kids don&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t RTFM, and developers these days tend not to appreciate the art of careful system design; they rather throw code at an IDE to see what sticks, if they don&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t vibe code it anyway.

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
New Wave Of Phishing Emails with SVG Files, (Tue, Jun 2nd)
items.description: For a few days, my SANS ISC mailbox is flooded with emails that delivers SVG files. An SVG ("Scalable Vector Graphic") is a web-friendly vector file format used for graphics and icons. No URL in the body, just â€œan imageâ€, that&#x27s the perfect way to deliver some malicious content. This isn&#x27t the first time that we see this technique used by threat actors[1].

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 2nd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9954, (Tue, Jun 2nd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 1st, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9952, (Mon, Jun 1st)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Unidentified RAT pushes NetSupport RAT, (Mon, Jun 1st)
items.description: Introduction

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
